data.title: Energy Price As An Economic Indicator
data.description:
What are energy prices telling us about the economy? A recent article on Bloomberg noted that:

“Hedge funds slashed their bullish position on crude to the lowest in about 17 years as risks of additional sanctions on Russian crude oil waned, bringing concerns about a global supply glut back to the fore. Money managers’ net-long position on West Texas Intermediate shrank by 19,578 lots to 29,686 lots in the week ended Tuesday, data from the Commodity Futures Trading Commission show. That’s the lowest since October 2008.”

However, there is more to this story than just easing global tensions and rising oil supplies impacting energy prices. Energy prices indicate economic strength, or, in this case, weakness. If the global economy grew strongly, the need for oil consumption would rise, absorbing the current production levels causing energy prices to rise. However, the outlook for economic growth in the major oil consumption economies, the U.S. and the Eurozone, is very weak.

This is also why we are seeing the fastest pace of Central Bank rate cuts since the pandemic crisis in 2020, and the Federal Reserve in the U.S. is now joining in despite record-high stock markets.

However, I would be remiss in not stating that energy prices are susceptible to "geopolitical shocks." This is because NYMEX traders bid up oil prices in anticipation of oil shortages, or conversely, excesses, but these price fluctuations tend to be shorter-term events.

Over the longer term, energy prices are an indicator of economic strength or weakness. This is because, as noted previously, energy is consumed in every part of the economic cycle.

“High oil prices add to the costs of doing business which pass, ultimately, on to customers and businesses. Whether it is higher cab fares, more expensive airline tickets, the cost of apples shipped from California, or new furniture shipped from China, high oil prices can result in higher prices for seemingly unrelated products and services.” – Investopedia

Of course, consumers who fill up their gas tanks each week immediately notice high oil prices. While core inflation reports strip out food and energy, those items drive short-term consumption patterns. Given that consumption comprises roughly 70% of the GDP calculation, the impact of higher oil prices is almost immediate.

As shown above, spikes in oil prices are highly correlated with economic recessions, financial events, and oil price reversions. Therefore, let’s explore energy prices as an economic indicator to see what oil is currently telling us about the state of the U.S. economy.

The Link To Oil

As noted, energy prices are crucial to the overall economic equation. As prices increase, consumers face higher inflationary costs. Unsurprisingly, there is a high correlation between the rise and fall of energy prices and the consumer price index. Unsurprisingly, energy prices and inflation have declined as economic demand weakened following the 2020-2021 stimulus-driven economic growth surge.

Since energy prices feed into virtually every aspect of our lives, from our food to the products and services we buy, we can expand our view of oil as an economic indicator. Therefore, the demand side of the equation is a tell-tale sign of economic strength or weakness. The chart below uses an economic composite of GDP, interest rates, and inflation compared to oil prices. It would be unsurprising that there is a decent correlation between the two.

Since the oil industry is manufacturing and production-intensive, falling energy prices impact other economically important facets of manufacturing, employment, and capital expenditures. The chart below shows oil prices and events relative to the deviation from the 4-year average energy price. While geopolitical events spike energy prices, as in 2022, those spikes result in economic downturns, reducing demand, thereby lowering energy prices.

While the pandemic-driven shutdown of the economy created a supply shortage, the flood of liquidity inevitably created a demand surge. That “pull-forward” of consumption led to surging inflationary pressures and rising oil prices. We show the high correlation between oil prices and breakeven inflation rates.

Oil prices reflect overall economic activity, given their vast contribution to everything consumed. With liquidity reversing, economic demand is weakening as the cost of living outpaces real wages. As such, the correlation between declining energy price, future economic growth, and inflation breakevens should be unsurprising.

The Fed Should Pay Closer Attention To The Energy Price

As noted above, global central banks have been cutting interest rates at the fastest pace since the pandemic, as many of those countries teeter on the edge of recession. However, the Federal Reserve remained steadfast until recently that its concern was a resurgence of inflation due to tariffs. But, as noted above, energy prices warned that inflation is not a concern for the U.S., and the Fed may be behind the curve in cutting rates.

Given that consumption makes up ~70% of the GDP calculation and the importance of oil in everything we consume, it should be unsurprising that oil prices also reflect economic activity in the aggregate. The current decline in energy prices says a lot about underlying economic strength, which the Fed noted has shown up in the latest employment data.

“In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate.” – J. Powell

However, as noted above, energy prices clearly show that inflation risks are tilted to the downside, with the larger risk being a much weaker economic growth rate. As such, the Federal Reserve should likely cut rates more aggressively now to avoid the potential warning from the decline in oil prices.

If energy prices reflect economic activity, then their current trend points to growing weakness beneath the surface of headline data. While equity markets reach new highs and unemployment appears low, the sharp drop in oil prices and declining breakeven inflation expectations tell a different story. It’s not geopolitics or supply excesses driving prices lower. It’s waning demand. And that’s a red flag.

The Fed’s delayed response to this signal is concerning. As Powell notes, their framework must balance inflation and employment. But right now, inflation pressures are easing, not accelerating. Oil prices reflect that shift more clearly than any lagging indicator. If consumption is slowing, and energy prices strongly suggest it is, then the Fed should move faster, not slower. Waiting for confirmation in backward-looking data risks compounding economic weakness already in motion.

Historically, when oil prices fall significantly, the economy follows. The Fed would do well to pay attention.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: A Historic Opportunity For Dividend Investors
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola for High Yield Investor.

The Fed just told us that they expect to soon "adjust their policy stance," given that the current policy is still in "restrictive territory."

They added that "the risks to employment have tilted to the downside", referring to how the economy is now weakening, at least in part due to the trade war.

They also seem to worry about the potential inflation caused by the tariffs, but this appears to be a secondary concern at this point.

This seems to strongly imply that they will soon start cutting interest rates once again.

That's not just my opinion. The debt market is now predicting a near 90% chance of a rate cut in just a few weeks from now:

And it shouldn't stop there. It is expected that interest rates will be cut two or three times by the December meeting:

Rate cuts should also continue into 2026, with the debt market predicting five cuts within a year from now:

This would bring interest rates to the low 3s, down from about 5% at their peak.

I think that this would be huge for dividend stocks.

Most of them have underperformed the broader market in recent years, in large part because the surge in interest rates caused capital to flow from dividend stocks into fixed-income investments.

It caused a lot of selling pressure as investors reallocated capital. REITs (VNQ) were hit particularly hard, but BDCs (BIZD), and traditional dividend stocks (SCHD) also underperformed the S&P500 (SPY) by a long shot:

But I now expect the exact opposite to happen as interest rates are cut and this same capital comes rushing back to dividend stocks.

Today, there is an enormous $7 trillion of cash on the sidelines. That's the most ever recorded:

Investors were comfortable leaving that capital in money-market funds and short-term treasuries when they offered a 5% yield, but as that's cut down to just about 3%, I expect many to reconsider their allocations.

There is just too big a difference.

When you earn a 5% yield, you feel like you are getting ahead. But after inflation and taxes, there is not much left if you are only earning a 3% yield.

Therefore, I expect a lot of this capital to get reallocated into higher-yielding dividend stocks, which could then push their valuations to much higher levels.

It is not uncommon to find even high-quality dividend stocks yielding 4, 5, 6, 7, or even up to 10% in the most extreme cases.

Today, you still have the chance to position yourself ahead of the crowd to lock in these higher yields and enjoy the upside of this catalyst.

Below, I highlight a number of compelling options, ranked from the lowest to the highest yielding opportunities:

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / OTCPK:BSRTF): 4.4% Dividend Yield

BSR is an apartment REIT that focuses on the rapidly growing Texas Triangle of Dallas, Austin, and Houston.

It owns affordable apartment communities in these markets and has historically enjoyed some of the fastest growth in its property sector:

The apartment sector faced some oversupply in recent years, which limited rent growth, but conditions are finally expected to improve as we go into 2026. The CEO of Camden Property Trust (CPT) just recently noted that he expects at least 4% rent growth in 2026 and 5% in 2027.

Even then, BSR is still today discounted, trading at a 25% discount to its net asset value, and the management is taking advantage of this low valuation to buy back shares. As rates are cut and the REIT's growth accelerates going into 2026, we expect the valuation to surge. While we wait, we earn a 4.4% dividend yield, and the REIT retains 40% of its cash flow to reinvest in growth.

Blue Owl Capital (OWL): 5% Dividend Yield

Blue Owl is one of the leading alternative asset managers. It is a close peer of Blackstone (BX), Brookfield (BAM), and KKR (KKR), but it is lesser-known, and it is causing it to trade at a lower valuation, despite enjoying qualities that warrant a premium.

Most importantly, the majority of its capital is permanent, meaning that it has no redemption plan. This greatly reduces risks and also accelerates long-term growth prospects as they won't deal with as much churn as other asset managers.

Moreover, they focus on investment niches that are rapidly growing in popularity. They are leaders in private credit and digital infrastructure, for example.

They have guided to grow their FRE per share by 20% annually going forward, which should fuel strong dividend growth.

Yet, you still have the chance to buy it at a 5% dividend yield. I suspect that as rates are cut and it keeps on growing, the market will eventually reprice it at a much lower yield, resulting in significant upside from here.

Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP / BEPC): 6% Dividend Yield

Brookfield Renewable is one of the biggest renewable energy infrastructure owners in the world. It enjoys long inflation-protected contracts that produce steadily growing cash flow and has a BBB+ rated balance sheet to keep funding new investments, earning a large spread over its cost of capital.

The company has guided to grow its FFO per share by 10% annually going forward, and yet, you still get to buy shares of the company at a 6% dividend yield.

Even ignoring any upside from repricing, investors have the chance to earn ~15% annual returns from the yield and growth alone. But as rates are cut, we expect the stock to eventually reprice at closer to a 4-5% dividend yield, which could lead to substantial upside.

InfraCap U.S. Preferred ETF (PFFA): 9.4% Dividend Yield

If you just want an ETF that should benefit from the rate cuts, then consider the InfraCap U.S. Preferred ETF

It is currently trading at ~10% discount to par value and offers a 9.4% yield, which is very attractive coming from preferred equity. They achieve this by investing in undervalued preferred shares and using a bit of leverage.

Therefore, they should benefit from both: the cost of their debt going down and more capital making its way to preferred equities.

It could allow them to hike the distribution and reach a higher share price as rates are cut.

Closing Note

I think that those investors who position themselves early in discounted high-yielding dividend stocks could enjoy quite substantial upside as interest rates are cut.

That's precisely what we are doing at High Yield Investor.

Valuations are still relatively low, yields are high, and the catalyst is imminent.

The window of opportunity could be closing.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Liberty Energy: A Hold In Current Market Conditions
data.description:
Introduction

In case you haven't noticed there are two oilfields in North America these days. On one side we have producers that have found ways to generate substantial operating cash flow to meet maintenance level capex, debt reduction, and shareholder returns at WTI prices in the low $60's. The stock prices of this group have rebounded somewhat from Liberation Day woes. Although stock prices are a bit higher, weaker WTI and gas prices have kept investors at bay during the quarter.

On the other side we have the service and supply industry-OFS. The stock prices of the driller and fracker subset of that group that depend largely on North American activity have largely remained eviscerated, at or below Liberation Day lows, as operators have continued to cut capex.

This is in spite of these companies having solid balance sheets with positive cash flow, manageable debt, cash reserves and ample liquidity through various credit facilities. Particularly in the case of Liberty Energy (NYSE:LBRT), America's largest independent fracker, and number two fracker overall.

In this article we will discuss the macro that's cratered the stock of Liberty, and what catalysts we might expect that would bring a rebound. Liberty reported their second quarter 2025 results late last month with a beat on the topline and a slight miss on EPS. The conference call had enough cautionary notes that the market has continued to "sell" LBRT to a 20% loss during the month of August. Withdrawing their prior guidance for the full year did nothing to assuage investors confidence.

We are calling a hold on LBRT at current levels as EPS projections are weaker in the remaining quarters of 2025.

Macro

There are three main drivers of the current state of affairs in the upstream industry. The unwinding of previous OPEC+ supply cuts has been putting 4-500 thousand barrels of crude back on the market at a time when U.S. shale is still cranking out at near all-time high levels, and new supply is on the horizon from the GOA, Brazil, and Guyana. All of this is leading the way into the time of the year that the refining industry normally curtails crude intake for maintenance. Yeesh! Market pundit, Eric Nuttall summarizes the supply situation fairly well in the X post below.

Then war premiums that had buffered oil prices in Q-1, began to dissipate in the second quarter, a trend that has continued into Q-3. When hostilities first break out or rhetoric becomes too inflamed, oil price ramp up in anticipation that infrastructure may come under attack. This seldom happens and the initial spike will fade over time.

There was also a lot of uncertainty in the market as regards tariffs. One quarter after their rollout, the market seems to be adapting as more countries enter into trade agreements with the U.S.

All of these factors have taken crude into the low to middle $60's, and analyst firm, Goldman Sachs, in a report cited by an OilPrice article, suggests they have farther to fall as we head into 2026. On the bullish side for crude we have new sanctions on India and the prospect of more severe sanctions on Russia directly.

There is one other macro that doesn't get a lot of ink as it has bullish implications for oil prices. According to a report put out by G&R Market Commentary, drilling and geologic productivity are moving in opposite directions. The report characterizes the decline in rock quality thusly-

Rigs have grown more efficient at turning steel into wellbores, yes—but each additional foot of wellbore delivers less oil than before. If the bottleneck were rigs, these efficiencies would send production soaring. But when the bottleneck is the dwindling stock of high-quality rock, faster rigs only brings decline on more quickly.

Thesis for LBRT

The company is a pure-play bet on North American energy. Not as pure as it used to be as they have a project in the Beetaloo basin in Australia, noted other potential projects in the Middle East and Argentina. There is a good chance international markets could develop meaningfully in the next few years as a number of countries are looking at unconventional reserves. America knows fracking the way no other country does and if these other countries want this technology it will definitely be exported. But international remains a future story for revenue impact.

LBRT also has investments in remote CNG power supply through its LPI subsidiary as well as Oklo Inc, (OKLO), and an investment in geothermal driller, Fervo Energy. Its March acquisition of IMG Solutions is already bearing fruit as CEO Ron Gusek noted in the call-

In Pennsylvania, we are collaborating with Range Resources and Imperial Land Corporation to provide power services to anchor a strategically located industrial park tailored for scalable development with advantaged access to Marcellus natural gas. In Colorado, our strategic alliance with AltitudeX Aviation Group will support a proposed development at the Colorado Air and Space Port powered by a Liberty Microgrid.

Liberty has its fingers in a lot of pies, but its mainstay is and will remain for the foreseeable future, tied to North American fracking.

It's anybody's guess as to when the domestic market will rebound. We maintain with activity now below replacement level for current production levels-NOVI Labs forecasts a 400K BOEPD drop by the end of next year,

At the current rig count of 540, energy analytics firm, Novi Labs forecasts a 400,000 bpd drop in lower 48 production by the end of next year, with losses upwards of 200,000 bpd within the first few months of 2026.

That day isn't too far in the future. A drop of that magnitude, and I think it could be larger, will be the long sought signal that the industry is going to have to pick up the pace to flatten the decline. This makes an investment in Liberty at this point in the cycle likely to be a good entry point.

Liberty is also paying investors to wait for better times with a modest dividend-yield 2.88%, and share (paused) buyback program.

Catalysts for LBRT

One of the core advancements the industry has made is in the area of precision pumping. This enables more accurate placement of sand in the reservoir and contributes to better completions with higher EUR's. LBRT's digiPrime fleets fields the industry's first variable speed natural gas reciprocating engine. These units provide precision rate control and increased torque, increasing both operational and capital efficiency.

Natural gas provides reduced wear and tear on engine components compared to diesel. Natural gas combustion also produces fewer particulates, extends oil life and significantly reduces engine stress, factors that contribute to engine lifespans expected to be 2 to 3x longer than conventional diesel and dual fuel-powered systems. CEO Gusek commented on how this is contributing to lower costs for the company-

We are already seeing this play out in the field. Digifleet power ends are lasting twice as long as conventional power ends while managing significantly higher load.

LBRT's high level of best in class pumping inventory should provide a structural advantage when the industry rebounds. Their LPI offering should also be leveraged in this scenario. Gusek commented here as well-

Within our power business, LPI delivers a robustly engineered end-to-end energy solution, uniquely integrating on-site generation and load management, ISO market participation and advantaged retail supply, creating a comprehensive flexible approach that redefines reliability and cost efficiency in deregulated regions.

There is a desire by operators to bundle services like this. It simplifies the operation and improves communication on site. I see this as a long term winner for LBRT.

Something else that is pretty slick is moving sand from a local mine to the frac site hydraulically. This takes trucks off the table which is very desirable. This is a needed innovation in this business. Stacking hundreds of trucks up is inefficient, contributes to air pollution, and makes the roadways more hazardous. Gusek's comments-

We also completed a successful field trial of the industry's first last mile sand slurry system. The system performed as designed, consistently exceeding delivery volumes in conjunction with our proprietary water handling system. We eliminate the truck traffic, the dust, the noise that comes with that. We give the truckers a more efficient time frame, and we basically remove all the road maintenance costs that come with all of that traffic on the road.

Companies will pay for innovation, and I wouldn't be surprised if this idea came from a LBRT foreman who just asked, "what if...". That's actually the way a lot of process improvements occur. The people doing the work catch the ear of a boss and do a napkin sketch. Heads nod up and down, and the boss says, "run with it. Let me know if it needs a push anyway in the organization." It can be just that simple. Anybody who pumps knows it's entirely feasible, heck, we are already pumping sand horizontally down hole, why not on the surface?

Q-2 and guidance

Q-2 revenue came to $1 billion compared to $977 million in the prior quarter, and increased 7% sequentially. Higher activity across nearly all business lines was able to offset pricing headwinds and softer conditions in the Permian market. Q-2 adjusted EBITDA was $181 million compared to $168 million in the prior quarter, up 8% sequentially. LBRT has a cash balance of $20 million and a net debt of $140 million. Net debt was reduced by $46 million from the prior quarter. LBRT paid $13 million in cash dividends, and decided to pause the share buybacks for the quarter based on market uncertainties.

Net capital expenditures were $134 million in the second quarter, and total capital expenditures for 2025 will be about $575 million. Some $75 million less than previously forecast.

Guidance

LBRT expects third quarter revenue and EBITDA to soften sequentially. EPS forecasts are $0.00 in Q-3, and a loss of $0.13 in Q-4. The company noted discounting from competitors as creating "pricing headwinds" in the quarter. Full year EBITDA guidance was withdrawn given the uncertainty in the market.

LBRT transcript

Risks

Realistically LBRT is range bound between $10-$13.00 per share until we get a price signal to go back to drilling. It's coming, but it's not here yet. Hence a hold rating on the stock.

Your takeaway

With the weakness in the outlook for North American activity for the rest of the year, I would not be adding to my small position in the company at current levels. It should be noted that this outlook can change pretty quickly in a scenario where crude rallies strongly. Investors interested in LBRT should pay close attention to the various drivers we mentioned in the beginning of this article, particularly a decline in shale output. Once the market perceives that monthly production is declining the sentiment around crude could improve rapidly. The Dec-19 $20.00 call is pretty cheap at $0.10 and is a way to hedge against some event shifting sentiment around North American OFS.

data.title: Potentially 12%-15% Consistent Income: Monthly Options Series (September 2025)
data.description:
Author's Note: This article is part of our periodic/monthly series that attempts to present two lists of stocks for the month that could be suitable for writing options to generate relatively safe income. Certain parts of the introduction, definitions, and section describing the selection process will have some commonality and repetitiveness with our previous articles in this series. This is unavoidable (as well as intentional) in keeping the entire series consistent and easy to follow for new readers. Such sections are presented in 'italics.' Regular readers who follow the series from month to month could skip such sections. Also, for more details on these sections, please refer to our BLOG-POST.

Why This Monthly Options Series?

Writing (or selling) options is not highly complicated, even though it does involve some learning curve and practice. Obviously, they are not for everyone. But if you are someone who is looking to potentially generate 12% to 15% income on a relatively consistent basis and is ready to put in the effort on a regular basis, we think you will find our monthly options series helpful. Option premiums are generally higher during periods of high volatility and vice versa. Also, premiums are higher for stocks that are more volatile compared to stocks that move in a tight range. They can also be impacted by earnings reports and/or the ex-dividend date falling during the option period.

Earning a decent income from your investments, significantly higher than the inflation rate, is always challenging. This has been especially true in the past decade and a half. In the last few years, the interest rates have been elevated, but in most cases, the fixed deposit rates barely match the inflation rate. Furthermore, interest rates may decline going forward, albeit at a slow pace. We believe selling options (cash-covered puts and covered calls or Buy-Write Calls) remain a relatively good choice for earning a high current income while conserving your investment capital. Obviously, there is a learning curve, and there are some risks involved with Options, and we do not recommend blindly jumping into the game. We will discuss how to mitigate the risks in a bit. Also, we will urge due diligence.

We also offer an Options-Income portfolio to our HIDIY subscribers, where we use roughly $75,000 to $100,000 of real-life money to write options on a regular basis.

All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article is sourced from Fidelity, Yahoo Finance, Barchart.com, and Investors.com.

Options Income Strategy 101

Note: This section is for readers who have not had much prior exposure to or experience with Options. We also describe the difference between 'Selling Options' versus 'Buying Options'. Please see our blog post (link provided above).

How To Mitigate the Risks

We do not intend to convey an impression, especially to the folks who are new to options, that there's no risk in selling options. In fact, there's plenty, especially if we're not careful. However, there are ways we can minimize the risk by following certain time-tested principles. We encourage you to read our blog post on SA, "How to mitigate risks with writing Options."

In brief, we cover the following in the above blog post:

Never use margin (or borrowed) money to sell/write options. Write options on stocks that you do not mind holding mid to long-term. Preferably, use dividend-paying underlying stocks so that if a call is assigned, you get the dividend while waiting for them to recover. Do not write Call options on stocks or positions that you do not want to lose (called away) under any circumstances. Do not chase very high premiums; 10-15% premiums (annualized) should be good enough.

Selection Strategy For Underlying Stocks

Note: This section describes the broader selection process and is repeated every month for the benefit of new readers. Regular readers could skip this and jump to the next section.

The market cap of the company is near or higher than $10 billion (this can be lowered somewhat in a down market). Daily volume for the underlying stock to be > 10,000. Dividend yield preferably > 1.5%; some exceptions are made.

By applying the above criteria, we get roughly 600 stocks.

We will calculate a "dividend safety score" based on factors like dividend history, past dividend growth, debt/capital, return on capital, credit rating, and EPS growth. Sure, a high safety score would not guarantee absolute safety because business conditions can change over time, new competition can emerge, or the management can get distracted or make some bad decisions, destroying shareholder value. Nonetheless, a high dividend safety score will at least provide a reasonable level of assurance that the company has the financial capability to continue making its dividend payments for the foreseeable future.

We're going to use our proprietary formulas (as detailed below) to calculate the optimal strike prices for CALL and PUT options. However, there are many other ways to determine the appropriate strike prices. You could use the Greek metrics, like delta, gamma, theta, etc., from your brokers' site to help select optimal strike prices. Readers are encouraged to try several methods before determining what works best for them. There are many other ways to determine the appropriate strike prices.

We will calculate the following ratios and factors:

The Distance ratio:

Distance-Ratio = (52-WK-HIGH - 52-WK-LOW)/((52-WK-HIGH + 52-WK-LOW)/2)

Distance-Ratio % = Distance-Ratio x 100

Strike-Price Safe Distance

Strike-Price-Safe-Distance % = [(Distance-Ratio %) x STPR-factor (STRIKE-PRICE-factor)] / 10

Whereas STPRC-factor = 1.2 (can vary from 1.0 to 1.5)

Note: The STPRC-Factor can be adjusted based on how volatile the underlying stock is. If the stock is highly volatile, the factor should be adjusted to a higher value like 1.5, whereas it can be set to a lower band like 1.2 (or less) for low-volatility stocks.

CALL Option Strike-price = Close-price + (Close-price x Strike-Price-Safe-Distance)

This price may need to be rounded to the lowest dollar or half-dollar amount, depending upon what strike prices are prevailing for the underlying stock for the specific strike date.

PUT Option Strike-price = Close-price - (Close-price x Strike-Price-Safe-Distance)

This price may need to be rounded up to the nearest dollar or half-dollar amount, depending upon what strike prices are prevailing for the underlying stock for the specific strike date.

There are many ways to determine the appropriate strike prices. However, we have chosen to use our above proprietary formulas to calculate the optimal strike prices for the sake of ease and simplicity.

Option Candidates For The Next Month

Below, we present two lists of 10 stocks each, one for writing PUT options and the other for writing CALL options. For CALL options, we buy the underlying stocks and sell deep-in-the-money calls at the same time. Our goal is simply to earn a high income (and not own these stocks), as, more than likely, those underlying shares would be called away. Please note that some stocks may appear in both lists as they may satisfy the criteria for more than one category.

10 Option Stocks Suitable For PUT Options

The market is close to its all-time high. Also, the candidates for the PUT options are biased toward momentum stocks, which means these stocks may be trading very close to their 52-week highs. If markets were to crash for any reason during the option period, we would likely get assigned these stocks. So, please use caution.

For PUT options, our primary objective is to generate income. We do not wish to hold these stocks as much as possible, so we would want to see them expire worthless. To identify such stocks, we select stocks that have had a rising trend recently. These stocks generally would have high relative strength and positive momentum. We will analyze the 1-week, 4-week, and 12-week price performance as well as relative strength data and try to see if the underlying stock is having a rising trend or a downward trend.

Momentum Score = (1Week-Perf)*2 + (4Week-Perf)*1 + (12Week-Perf)/2

The above formula gives higher weightage to more recent momentum.

We sort the list based on the momentum score and cross-check with the relative strength and composite rating ( sourced from IBD - Investor Business Daily, subscription required). Also, we try to avoid stocks with very high market prices (above $350 per share) as the cost per option contract becomes prohibitive, especially for folks with limited capital. However, some exceptions are made at times. We finally selected ten stocks for PUT options.

If the outcome of the option is not favorable at expiry, and the option does get assigned (we will be put the shares in such cases), the rising trend will help write a fresh CALL option immediately, possibly with a good premium.

In our list of 10 candidates, we should be careful not to put too many names from the same industry segment. We generally limit to two names from the same sector to avoid too much concentration in one sector.

A word of caution on PUT options: Do not start a PUT option on a stock that you do not see yourself holding for an extended period of time. Also, please note that this list only highlights probable good candidates, but further due diligence may be required.

Here are the top 10 large-cap stocks for PUT options:

(APH), (AVGO), (VST), (ORCL), (HWM), (WWD), (NRG), (GILD), (HEI), and (ADM).

Please note that we have generally excluded stocks that have a share price greater than $350 a share. At times, one or two exceptions are made.

Table 1:

Below, we present the current PUT Options trades and likely current premiums that we can expect for the above ten stocks.

Table 1A:

Please note that the premiums for PUT options are reasonable this month, especially if you add the interest earned on the cash reserve set aside for the options. We have taken the option prices during the weekend when the market was closed, so the bid/ask spreads were a bit wider. The option prices can change quickly based on the market sentiments and direction of the underlying stock. Our average discount (distance from the current price to the strike price) is relatively good, at roughly -10% (on average).

CAUTION: Please note that this month, we have only one stock with its earnings report due during the option period. Any surprises in the earnings report can cause volatility in the stock price. Earnings releases can cause surprises on the positive or negative side, adding extra risk to the trade. Please use due diligence.

10 Option Stocks With Relatively Safe Dividends For BUY-WRITE Calls:

In this category, our selection criteria are quite different than those for PUT options in the first category. In this set, the emphasis is on the quality of the stock rather than high recent momentum. We will use our dataset, especially the dividend-related data, to calculate a score called the "Dividend Safety and Quality Score." However, there's nothing that we can claim to be absolutely safe in the investing world, so it is only on the basis of relative safety. We use various financial metrics, such as payout ratios, debt-to-capital ratios, S&P credit rating, top-line growth, and dividend growth over the years. Based on the above factors and EPS rating, we calculate the dividend safety or quality score.

In this category, we will use "DEEP-In-The-Money" BUY-WRITE call options. It basically involves two (simultaneous) legs to the option contract; first, we buy the underlying stock (100 shares for one contract) at the current market price, and in the second leg, we sell a CALL option at a strike price that is nearly 7%-12% below the current market price. During times of high market volatility, we may even go for even higher discounts. In fact, both legs are traded at the same time, and your cost basis is the market price minus the premium you received. This cost basis is what you use in your limit orders. Your final cost basis may further be reduced by any dividend that you may earn/receive during the option period. If you already happen to own the stock from before, you can only use the second leg. Since our cost basis is usually 7-12% below the current market prices, this method offers an additional margin of safety.

We present ten such companies with high dividend safety scores. With these stocks, besides an average of 1-2% dividend, you can safely generate 10-15% additional income by writing covered-call options. The key is not to be greedy with premiums.

Our Top 10 Stocks with relatively safe dividends for (BUY-WRITE) CALL options are:

(HEI), (V), (AMAT), (MSFT), (APH), (RGLD), (LRCX), (CTAS), (HWM), and (JPM).

Please note that we have excluded most stocks that have a share price greater than $250 a share. However, we have made a couple of exceptions this time.

Table-2:

Note: 1) The "Dividend Rating" (shown as "Quality Rating") is based on recent past parameters like dividend growth (1-year, 3-year, and 5-year), number of years of dividend growth, Payout Ratio based on cash flow (or EPS), ROC (Return on Capital), Sales growth, Debt/capital, Credit Rating, and EPS Rating.

2) It's very important to know how much capital you're willing to commit and if you can really afford to allocate it. This strategy should only be used with stocks that you do not mind owning and holding for an extended period of time (short to mid-term).

(HEI), (V), (AMAT), (MSFT), (APH), (RGLD), (LRCX), (CTAS), (HWM), and (JPM).

Table 3:

CAUTION: Please note that this month, the premiums for PUT options are slightly lower than the deep ITM calls, but are still reasonable. The earnings season is mostly over, and only a couple of companies in our list have earnings releases during the Option period. All of the options have their expiration date set to Sep-19th, which is 25 days away at the time of writing.

Note: 1. Some of the Option prices have a wide bid/ask spread. In such cases, we have taken the mid-range prices based on bid/ask, and sometimes, lower than mid-range prices, but there is no guarantee that those prices would fill at all times. It is always recommended to use limit orders only. Also, during open market hours, Option prices can fluctuate rapidly based on the price movement of the underlying stock.

2. If there are multiple stocks from the same industry segment, it will be best to use only one of them to avoid concentration risk.

Conclusion

Please carefully review the goals of each of the distinct strategies. We think these lists offer great selections for writing/selling PUT or CALL (or BUY-WRITE) options. As usual, we have tried to put relatively safe stocks in both groups; however, the stocks listed in the second list for call options (or buy-write call options) have been specifically filtered based on the safety of their dividends. Please note that nothing is absolutely safe in the investing world.

Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.39% (Institutional shares) and 2.44% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • Strong issuer and sector selection, especially among B and CCC rated securities, and in the food & beverage and diversified manufacturing sectors, boosted performance. Index products, including high yield exposure, also helped, while the health care and retail sectors detracted.
  • The fund had an up-in-quality position, with overweight positions in BB and B1 rated securities, and underweight holdings in B3 and CCC rated debt. Sector tilts favored diversified manufacturing and aerospace & defense, while cyclicals were avoided. Liquid instruments, such as exchange-traded funds and total return swaps, enhanced the fund’s efficiency.
Contributors Detractors
Strong issuer selection in B and CCC rated credit was a key driver of positive relative performance, as the fund benefited in rising markets while it avoided weaker names. Sector allocation also contributed, with food & beverage and diversified manufacturing the standout performers. These sectors benefited from favorable market conditions and well- chosen holdings. The fund’s use of index products, including modest high yield exposure, added value. By sector, health care and retailers were the main detractors, as both faced headwinds and performed weakly. Security selection in these areas did not offset broader sector weakness. The fund’s income return slightly lagged the benchmark, which partially offset gains from price appreciation and other positive contributors.

Further insight

Bank loan spreads rallied to about 400 basis points (bps) after peaking at 475bps amid volatility early in the quarter, driven by strong technical factors, resilient fundamentals, and shifting macroeconomic expectations. While credit spreads appear rich historically, they remain attractive relative to other risk assets. Loans offer high 7% coupons and near-zero duration (interest rate sensitivity), providing compelling income and rate protection. Risks in the second half of this year include geopolitical instability, tariff-driven volatility, and a mixed growth/inflation outlook. data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Caterpillar Sees Bigger Tariff Hit
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Caterpillar raised the expected annual and quarterly impact of tariffs on its results. (0:15) Marvell Technology slumps on disappointing outlook. (0:58) Venture capitalist to be interim head of CDC. (2:02)

The following is an abridged transcrtipt:

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) is boosting its expected impact of tariffs.

Shares are under pressure premarket after the company said it now expects the net impact from tariffs to be $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion this year, up from a previous estimate of $1.3 billion to $1.5 billion. For Q3 it sees an impact of $500 million to $600 million, up from earlier outlook of $400 million to $500 million.

Including the net impact from incremental tariffs, Caterpillar said in filing that it now expects its full-year adjusted operating profit margin to come in near the bottom of the target margin range. But it left its full-year sales outlook unchanged.

"While the company continues to take initial mitigating actions to reduce this impact, trade and tariff negotiations continue to be fluid," the company said.

In a further worry to the AI trade, Marvell Technology (MRVL) is tumbling before the bell following disappointing revenue guidance.

For the quarter in progress, Marvell expects a net revenue midpoint of $2.06 billion, less than the $2.11 billion consensus. Adjusted EPS for the third quarter is forecast at $0.74, just above the estimate of $0.73.

For fiscal Q2, the semiconductor company reported adjusted EPS of $0.67, which mirrored the consensus estimate of $0.67. Revenue for the quarter came in at $2.006 billon, which just missed the estimate of $2.01 billion.

Q2 revenue jumped 58% year over year. The gains were led by its data center segment, which surged 69% year over year to $1.49 billion.

But data center revenue is expected to be flat sequentially in Q3.

On the earnings call, CEO Matthew Murphy said “lumpiness” in the custom design business is “normal to see, particularly with the large hyperscale builds that happen and especially as you ramp them into production, which we've done this year on a number of programs.”

Investors “should expect a strong fourth quarter for custom," he added.

And reports say the White House has picked Jim O'Neill, the deputy secretary at Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.'s Department of Health and Human Services, to serve as interim CDC director.

The top CDC post was vacated after the White House said Director Susan Monarez was removed from that office.

O'Neill has an investor background. He was CEO of the Thiel Foundation from 2009 to 2012 and managing director at venture capital firm Mithril Capital Management from 2012 to 2019. Although he has never worked at CDC nor has a public health background, O'Neill has served at HHS in prior Republican administrations. Roles included associate deputy secretary and principal associate deputy secretary.

The AP says a flashpoint is expected in the coming weeks as a key advisory committee, which Kennedy has reshaped with vaccine skeptics, is expected to issue new recommendations on immunizations. The panel is scheduled to review standard childhood shots for measles, hepatitis and other diseases.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Alibaba (BABA) reported quarterly earnings that missed on the top and bottom lines.

UnitedHealth (UNH) is under pressure for loans it made to healthcare providers after its cyberattack.

And NBC will offer its Peacock Plus streaming service on Amazon Prime.

Taking a look at the markets, stock index futures are giving some back after the S&P 500 closed above 6,500 for the first time. Tech is weighing after Marvell’s results. In the Treasury market, yields are slightly higher across the curve ahead of the release of Fed’s favorite inflation gauge.

And on the economic calendar today:

  • 8:30 a.m. ET – The July Personal Income and Spending report arrives. That comes with the aforementioned Fed’s favorite inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, which is expected to have risen 0.3% on the month.
  • 8:30 a.m. ET – July Wholesale and Retail Inventories.
  • 9:45 a.m. ET – August Chicago PMI.
  • 10:00 a.m. ET – August Michigan Consumer Sentiment Final.

data.title: Amazon: Snatch Up This Deep Value Growth Stock Now
data.description:
As an investor, my objective is simple: Buy the most qualitative businesses on the planet.

This is done because of the idea that only a select few stocks drive all of the wealth created in the stock market. More specifically, research by Arizona State University professor Hendrik Bessembinder backs up this claim.

Bessembinder found that from 1926 through 2019, approximately 4% of more than 26,000 stocks accounted for all of the wealth creation in the stock market over that time. Strikingly, the remaining 96% of stocks overall only matched the performance of one-month T-bills.

My focus today will be on the retail e-commerce and cloud computing heavyweight Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN)(TSX:AMZN:CA). When I last covered the stock with a strong buy rating in May, I appreciated the role that it plays in our daily lives. I also thought that the shift in shift playing out in global IT spending was a growth catalyst. In my view, the company's AA-rated balance sheet was another plus. Relative to my fair value estimate, shares of the stock were also an undeniable value.

Three months later, I'm reaffirming my strong buy rating. Putting my money where my keyboard is, I'm also going to be adding to my stake in the coming days.

That's because global IT spending allocations could be flipped on their head in the next 10 to 15 years. As the leader in cloud computing, Amazon is strategically positioned to keep cashing in on this secular tailwind. The company's leading e-commerce platform is poised to benefit from industry catalysts, too. Amazon's balance sheet remains robust as well. Finally, the stock is still trading at a sizable discount to my fair value estimate.

Amazon Is Firing On All Cylinders

On July 31st, Amazon shared its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's net sales jumped 13.3% year-over-year to $167.7 billion during the quarter. For more perspective, this topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by $5.6 billion.

Unsurprisingly, Amazon displayed strength throughout its business for the second quarter. The North America segment led the way again, with net sales climbing 11.1% over the year-ago period to $100.1 billion during the quarter. Just like in previous quarters, increased unit sales, advertising sales, and subscription services were credited.

Greater unit sales were again fueled by the trifecta of Amazon's commitment to providing customers with competitive prices, an extensive selection of goods, and convenience.

On the price front, Amazon's value proposition remains industry-leading. CEO Andy Jassy noted in his opening remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that research firm Profitero has concluded that the company has the lowest prices of any U.S. retailer. This marked the eighth consecutive year the research firm has had this finding.

Amazon has also been working diligently to expand its product selection even further. In recent months, the company has secured the return of Nike's products to the retail store. Amazon has also added premium brands like Aveda and Marc Jacobs Fragrances.

The company has also worked to improve its delivery speeds. In Q2 2025, Amazon delivered 30% more items to Prime members in the U.S., same day or next day, than in Q2 2024.

Perhaps, it's no surprise then that this year's Prime Day last month was another smashing success. It was Amazon's biggest ever with record sales, number of items sold, and Prime sign-ups in the 3 weeks leading up to the event.

The company's International segment posted $36.8 billion in net sales in the second quarter. That was a 16.1% year-over-year growth rate. This outsized growth was largely for the same reasons as the double-digit percentage topline growth rate in the North America segment.

The AWS segment recorded $30.9 billion in net sales for the second quarter, which was up 17.5% over the year-ago period. This was driven by increased customer usage during the quarter, which more than offset pricing changes driven by long-term customer contracts.

Amazon's diluted EPS rocketed 35.5% higher year-over-year to $1.68 in the second quarter. That surpassed the analyst consensus for the quarter by $0.35. This stemmed from a 170-plus basis point expansion in the net profit margin to 10.8% during the quarter.

The big picture remains quite promising for Amazon on the cloud computing front.

CEO Andy Jassy reiterated that 85% to 90% of worldwide IT spend remains on-premises versus in the cloud. Over the next 10 to 15 years, he believes this will flip, with adoption being accelerated by the appetite for leveraging AI.

The future for the AWS segment remains bright. On a company-specific level, demand remains exceptional for Amazon. Fellow SA analyst Yiannis Zourmpanos noted in this article that the company's AWS backlog soared 25% over the year-ago period to $195 billion in the quarter. As long-term trends continue to play out and Amazon works to resolve supply constraints, growth should remain healthy for AWS.

Trends in the global retail e-commerce market should be another driver for Amazon. Grand View Research anticipates that the global retail e-commerce market will compound by 11.6% annually from $5.9 trillion in 2023 to $12.3 trillion by 2030.

As the company continues to enhance the customer experience with low prices, an expanding selection, and faster shipping times, there's every reason to believe it can keep winning share.

This is why I believe that the FAST Graphs analyst growth consensus for operating cash flow per share is realistic. For 2025, the current projection is for OCF per share to rise by 21.3% to $13.11. In 2026, an additional 23.7% spike in OCF per share to $16.21 is expected. For 2027, another 22.7% increase in OCF per share to $19.90 is being predicted.

Still Churning Out Free Cash Flow With A Powerful Balance Sheet

Predictably, Amazon's rapid acceleration in capex spending caused TTM free cash flow to drop 65.7% year-over-year to $18.2 billion as of Q2 2025. Given that the company has many growth avenues, it makes sense that it's aggressively investing in its future. If/when Amazon chooses to flip the free cash flow switch, I don't doubt that it can do so with ease.

Financially, Amazon's net cash and cash equivalents/marketable securities continue to grow. As of June 30th, 2025, this balance was $42.5 billion. This was up sequentially from $41.2 billion in Q1 2025. Consequently, Amazon earns an AA credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook (unless otherwise noted or hyperlinked, all details in the prior two subheads were from Amazon's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, Amazon's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing, and Amazon's Q2 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Keeps Climbing Higher

In the last three months, Amazon's shares have generated a 12% total return. That's just ahead of the 10% total return logged by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Amazon's outperformance looks like it could be just beginning, too. That's because the stock remains objectively cheap based on the valuation metric in which it tracks most in line. Of course, I'm referring to Amazon's valuation on an operating cash flow basis.

The stock's forward 12-month P/OCF ratio is only 15.1. Even after its recent rally, this is still almost four standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 23.8, per FAST Graphs.

As I have discussed in previous articles, I believe that a fair value P/OCF ratio for Amazon is 21.4. This would be one standard deviation under the 10-year average multiple.

Why am I reasonably confident that this represents Amazon's fair value P/OCF ratio?

Well, the company's annual OCF per share growth consensus of 22.7% remains close to its 10-year average OCF per share CAGR of around 27%. When adjusting for the sheer size of Amazon now versus five or 10 years ago, it makes sense that percentage growth is slowing. In the grand scheme of things, it's not by all that much, though.

This suggests that Amazon's growth profile remains largely intact. Thus, I believe that a P/OCF ratio of 21.4 appropriately reflects this fact.

Currently, the calendar year 2025 is 66% complete. That means another 34% of 2025 and 66% of 2026 lie ahead in the coming 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month OCF per share input of $15.14.

Plugging this into my fair value P/OCF ratio of 21.4, I compute a fair value of $324 a share. Compared to the current $229 share price (as of August 27th, 2025), that works out to be a 29% discount to fair value.

If Amazon meets growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could have a 45% upside through September 2026. Over the next five years, the stock could put up a cumulative total return of 217% - - 24% annualized.

Risks To Consider

Amazon enjoys a vigorous growth outlook. Even so, the company does have risks that could alter or undo the investment thesis.

One risk to Amazon is its dominance in U.S. retail e-commerce. The most recent data from Business Dasher shows the company's share of the market is approaching 40% (37.8%). That's magnitudes higher than Walmart's (WMT) 6.3% market share.

This level of superiority is a double-edged sword. On one hand, I love to see my investment holdings tower over competitors. This serves as a testament to Amazon's ability to deliver positive experiences that routinely bring customers back for more.

On the other hand, regulators may not see it this way. The risk is that if a legal challenge arose and Amazon didn't prevail, the company could be broken into separate entities. That could interfere with Amazon's ecosystem and potentially hurt its growth potential.

As I noted in May, another risk to the company is the potential for a cyber breach. Amazon's scores of sensitive data make it the target of countless attempted breaches. If any find widespread success, this could result in sensitive stakeholder data being jeopardized. That could prompt lawsuits and fines from regulators, possibly harming Amazon's brand image and market share.

One last risk to the company is that it is economically sensitive. Economic slowdowns can temporarily weigh on growth prospects and pressure the stock's sentiment. This happened in 2022, when OCF per share only grew by 2% to $4.59.

The Kalshi betting odds for a recession in 2025 have dropped from 47.9% at the start of this year (and a peak near 70% in April) to just 10% now. Still, it's worth noting.

Summary: I'm Buying More Amazon Next Week

The continued shift in IT spending from on-premises to the cloud bodes well for Amazon. As does the ongoing surge in global retail e-commerce. This is why I still think the company has a lengthy runway for double-digit percentage OCF per share growth. Amazon's significant net cash and cash equivalents/marketable securities position adds to its appeal. Shares also remain firmly discounted, which is leading me to stand with my strong buy rating.

I have added to my Amazon position five times in 2025. This has helped the stock to become my second-biggest position behind Nvidia (NVDA), at a 5.8% weighting. As a sign of my conviction, I'm going to be boosting my Amazon stake by nearly another 10% to begin next month.

data.title: BlackRock High Yield V.I. Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted a return of 3.93% (Class I shares) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s performance was boosted by strong allocation to and selection in the technology, retailers, and midstream sectors, as well as high yield bonds. Detractors included an underweight exposure to the transportation services sector, selection in the media and other industries sectors, and out-of-benchmark exposures to term loans, convertibles, and BBB rated securities.
  • The fund had an underweight exposure to BB rated securities and an overweight allocation to B rated securities. The bank loan holding amounted to 7.5% of the fund. From a sector standpoint, the fund had overweight allocations to property & casualty, technology, and aerospace & defense, and underweight positions in retailers, cable & satellite, and media & entertainment.
Contributors Detractors
An overweight allocation to and security selection in the technology sector boosted performance. Security selection in the retailers and midstream sectors also contributed positively. Security selection in core high yield bonds enhanced returns. Out-of-benchmark allocations to high yield index products and security selection in BB rated securities provided further support. An underweight allocation to the transportation services sector detracted the most from performance. Security selection in the media & entertainment and other industry sectors also hurt returns. Out-of-benchmark allocations to term loans and convertibles products were detrimental. From a ratings perspective, an out-of-benchmark allocation to BBB rated securities was the largest detractor.

Further insight

We expect elevated dispersion to persist in the second half of this year, driven by macroeconomic uncertainty, evolving trade policy, and shifting central bank expectations. We see ample opportunity for boosting performance through active credit selection. While average spreads remain tight, all-in yields above 7% continue to offer attractive “carry” and a meaningful buffer against downside scenarios. This backdrop, combined with solid fundamentals and a constructive technical setup, reinforces the case for maintaining high yield exposure.

data.title: Buyers Strike: Pending Home Sales Drop Further, Plunge In Midwest, Near Record Lows In All Regions As Supply Balloons
data.description:
And to top it off: cancellations of pending sales rose to a record rate in July.

Demand in the housing market sagged further: Pending home sales dropped by 0.4% in July from June, seasonally adjusted. They have now spent nearly three years crawling along, or setting, record lows, according to data going back to 2010 from the National Association of Realtors on Thursday (historical data in the chart via YCharts):

It’s even worse: the cancellation rate. Pending sales are based on contract signings and track deals that haven’t closed yet and could still get canceled because buyers cannot afford or even get homeowner’s insurance, or for other reasons. Signed contracts that then get cancelled are included in the pending sales here, but are not included in closed sales reported later.

Alas, Redfin reported that in July, 58,000 pending sales were canceled, amounting to 15.3% of all pending sales - the highest rate for any July in Redfin’s data going back to 2017.

Pending sales in all regions near record lows. Pending sales fell sharply in the Midwest, but also declined in the South and Northeast. The West was the only region where pending sales rose. Pending sales remained near record lows in all four regions.

Pending sales of existing homes in the US, compared to the Julys in prior years:

  • 2024: +0.7%
  • 2023: -6.5%
  • 2022: -21%
  • 2021: -36%
  • 2020: -41%
  • 2019: -32%

The buyers’ strike continues because prices are too high after shooting up by 50% or more within 2-3 years. Those prices no longer make economic sense.

While inventory surged. This plunge in demand coincides with surging inventories of existing homes for sale - despite large waves of delistings by frustrated sellers - leading to the most supply since July 2016 over the past three months, along with May 2020.

Split out by single-family homes and condos: Supply of single-family homes rose to 4.5 months, while supply of condos has been over 6 months since April, the highest since 2012 at the end of the Housing Bust. Here is the combined supply:

In the South, pending sales dipped 0.1% month-to-month, seasonally adjusted.

A map of the four census regions is posted in the comments below.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: +1.8%
  • 2023: -9%
  • 2020: -39%
  • 2019: -30%

But inventories surged across the South.

In Texas, inventory of existing homes for sale in July jumped by 24% year-over-year, and by 32% from July 2019, to the highest in at least the decade of data from Realtor.com (we discussed the inventories of the big metros in Texas here).

In Florida, active listings in July jumped by 23% year-over-year, and by 29% from July 2019, according to data from Realtor.com (we discussed Florida’s by-metro inventories and days on the market here):

And, for example, in the Atlanta metropolitan area, active listings spiked by 31% year-over-year in July:

In the West, pending sales rose by 3.7% in July from June, which had been the second-lowest on record.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: -1.9%
  • 2023: -6%
  • 2020: -47%
  • 2019: -40%

Active listings in the West have also been surging.

In Los Angeles County and in the San Francisco metropolitan area, active listings reached the highest level for any July in the decade of data from Realtor.com (discussion of inventories in the biggest markets of California is here).

In other markets in the West, inventories have also surged, reaching the highest for any July in the decade of data in the metros of Denver, Seattle, Salt Lake City, Phoenix, Tucson, Portland, and Las Vegas (discussion by metro here).

For example, active listings in the Denver-Aurora-Centennial metro are up by 48% from July 2019 (dotted purple), and along with June, the most in the decade of data from Realtor.com.

In the Northeast, pending sales dipped by 0.6% month-to-month, seasonally adjusted.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: -0.6%
  • 2023: -2%
  • 2020: -42%
  • 2019: -31%

In the Northeast, inventories have also grown, but remain tighter than in other parts of the US.

For example, in the Boston-Cambridge-Newton metro, inventory jumped by 25% year-over-year and by 61% from July 2023, to be roughly level with July 2020 but well below the prepandemic years.

In the Midwest, pending sales dropped by 4.0% in July from June, seasonally adjusted, to the second-lowest sales in the data going back to 2010.

The record low had been July 2024, and compared to this record low, sales were 1.3% higher.

Compared to the Julys of prior years:

  • 2024: +1.3%
  • 2023: -8%
  • 2020: -39%
  • 2019: -29%

Inventories in some of the big metros in the Midwest diverge, rising sharply in some, such as in the metro of Columbus, OH, but less so in the metros of Detroit and Chicago:
